title: Reservation authorization and capacity workbench for Dedicated pools
preview:
Goal: Define and implement a bounded, auditable reservation request and approval workflow plus an operator workbench that ties throughput guarantees, autoscaling policy templates, and fleet controls into the Dedicated provisioning lifecycle. This ticket scopes the PM deliverables: request form schema, approval matrix, capacity planning heuristics, UX sketches for the workbench, and the verification playbook for provisioning events.

Context: Several early customers are adopting Dedicated and ask for a single place to request guaranteed throughput (tokens/sec or requests/sec), choose autoscaling behaviors (aggressive, balanced, conservative), and indicate fleet constraints (allowed regions, GPU families, maintenance windows). Engineers want predictable capacity sizing and test hooks to validate SLOs after an allocation. Current ad-hoc requests cause back-and-forth between sales, SRE, and infra and increase lead time.

Scope (what we will deliver in this ticket):
- Definitive reservation request schema (fields, validations, default values).
- Approval matrix that maps request attributes (size, contract value, security needs) to required approvers and SLAs.
- Autoscaling policy templates (3 presets + custom) with recommended defaults for GPU pool sizes, target utilization, cooldowns, burst capacity and drain behavior.
- Fleet control knobs and constraints (tagging, allowed racks/regions, maintenance windows, preemption policy).
- Capacity planning heuristics and forecasting doc to convert expected QPS/seq_len/traffic profile into GPU unit reservation and cost estimate.
- Operator workbench wireframes and flows (review queue, approve/deny with comments, rollback, test harness link).
- Verification & onboarding checklist (smoke tests, perf benchmarks, KV cache warmup, canary traffic ramp).

Out of scope: billing contract language, long-term SLA legal framework, deep infra implementation (this ticket produces the product spec and acceptance tests; ENG tickets will follow).

title: Contributor stewardship, deprecation & retirement playbook for design system
preview: Establish a single, pragmatic playbook for accepting contributions to the design system, lifecycle review for components and tokens, and a safe deprecation/retirement process. This ticket consolidates prior ad-hoc guidance into a playbook that: 1) makes contribution expectations clear, 2) defines an RFC + review cadence for breaking changes, 3) defines telemetry and usage thresholds for safe retirement, and 4) gives migration guidance for tokens and components. Goal: reduce friction for contributors while making risk visible to product and infra stakeholders.
text: Establish a single, pragmatic playbook for accepting contributions to the design system, lifecycle review for components and tokens, and a safe deprecation/retirement process. This ticket consolidates prior ad-hoc guidance into a playbook that: 1) makes contribution expectations clear, 2) defines an RFC + review cadence for breaking changes, 3) defines telemetry and usage thresholds for safe retirement, and 4) gives migration guidance for tokens and components. Goal: reduce friction for contributors while making risk visible to product and infra stakeholders.

title: Synthetic Load Validator and Reservation Autocalibration Playbook
preview:
Summary:
During a capacity incident on 2025-02-10 we observed degraded throughput across several Dedicated clusters. Investigation pointed to a combination of: (1) under-provisioned tenant reservations due to stale traffic estimates, (2) noisy-neighbor workloads that generated burst token exhaustion, and (3) burst policy thresholds that allowed aggressive short-term bursts without adequate backoff, leading to cascading latency spikes.

Goal:
Create a reproducible synthetic load validator and an automated reservation autocalibration playbook that: (a) reproduces the incident class with controlled noisy tenants and realistic KV/cache utilization, (b) derives corrective reservation deltas, and (c) produces safe degraded-burst fallback policies that reduce tail latency without unduly impacting throughput.

Scope and non-goals:
- Scope: Dedicated cluster behavior, tenant reservation sizing, burst token lifecycle, telemetry augmentations, runbook changes, CI gating for reservation changes.
- Non-goals: Changes to customer-visible pricing, on-prem deployment packaging, or host-level kernel changes.

Incident timeline (high level):
- 2025-02-10 02:14 UTC: Alert: sustained 99th pct latency > 2.5s across cluster-dedicated-12.
- 02:18 UTC: Autoscaler attempted to add capacity but reservation rebalancer was delayed by lock contention.
- 02:23 UTC: Several tenants reported 429s when bursts were consumed; tokenbucket metrics show exhaustion event spikes.
- 02:45 UTC: Manual mitigation: soft-fence noisy tenants and temporarily increase reservation of impacted tenants.

Root causes (synthesized):
- Reservation mismatch: historic quota computation used an exponentially weighted moving average tuned for steady-state, underreacting to sudden growth of 2.5x in a top tenant's QPS.
- Burst policy gap: burst allowance (token refill rate) was permissive but lacked graceful decay; no intermediate throttling step between full allowance and hard throttles.
- Telemetry blind spots: synthetic replay tests were not run for cross-tenant KV cache eviction patterns which amplified per-request cost.
- Operational contention: reservation rebalance run serialized under global lock; during incident it queued behind long-running topology reconciliation.

Proposed action items (short, medium, long):
1) Short (this sprint):
  - Build \"synthetic-load-validator\" harness that can emulate: multi-tenant steady + burst traffic, KV cache pressure, variable sequence lengths, and controlled noisy behavior. (Owner: ENG-ops)
  - Add tokenbucket-exhaustion alert with correlated reservation-change events in Grafana and link to runbook. (Owner: Observability)
  - Implement emergency soft-fence API: quick-apply tenant isolation mode that limits new bursts for a tenant while preserving inflight inference. (Owner: runtime)
2) Medium (next 2 sprints):
  - Autocalibration algorithm: forecast-driven reservation adjustment using short-window percentile (P90 over 10m + burst headroom) plus hysteresis and cooldown. Provide dry-run outputs in staging for 24h before primetime. (Owner: scheduler)
  - Replace global rebalance lock with per-pool coordination and deadline-based preemption to avoid queueing. (Owner: allocator)
  - Add synthetic test step to CI that runs a 30m noisy-neighbor scenario and validates no 99th-pt uplift beyond baseline. (Owner: testing infra)
3) Long (Q2+):
  - Policy simulation tooling to model cluster-level fairness and revenue impact of reservation deltas. (Owner: capacity-analytics)
  - Explore tiered burst decay policies (soft-throttle -> token-rate-reduction -> hard-throttle) with configurable knobs per customer tier. (Owner: runtime/policy)

Acceptance criteria:
- Synthetic harness reproduces observed tokenbucket exhaustion within 10% of incident metrics when fed historical traffic profile.
- Autocalibration performs reservation adjustments that reduce 99th-pt latency by >= 30% in the noisy scenario while keeping overall throughput within 5% of baseline.
- Emergency soft-fence can be applied in < 90 seconds and documented in the runbook.
- CI synthetic test runs in under 40 minutes and fails the PR if 99th-pt latency or reservation drift regression occurs.

Design notes and trade-offs:
- Forecasting approach: choose between reactive short-window percentile (less aggressive) vs ML model (more accurate but operationally heavier). We'll begin with deterministic percentile+cooldown for safety, with a supervised ML model as future work.
- Synthetic fidelity: running full-scale GPU-backed synthetic tests is expensive. We'll use a tiered strategy: simulated CPU-only high-level load for quick CI checks, and nightly full-GPU clusters in staging for high-fidelity validation.
- Soft-fence semantics: design to avoid disrupting in-flight requests by throttling new token grants; this reduces blast radius while preserving ongoing responses.

Runbook and CI changes:
- Add step in Dedicated Incident runbook: \"Apply soft-fence to tenant -> verify tokenbucket metrics -> run synthetic validator in local-mode\". Include rollback steps for soft-fence.
- CI: add job `noisy_neighbour_smoke` that executes the lightweight harness and validates P95/P99 and reservation stability.

Telemetry and dashboards to build:
- Grafana: \"Reservation Autocalibrator\" dashboard: per-tenant reservation, estimated desired reservation, tokenbucket fill-rate, exhaustion events, KV-eviction rate.
- Alerting: new composite alert combining tokenbucket_exhaustion OR reservation_delta > 3x within 5m, with pager if customer_impact==high.

Owners and collaborators:
- Runtime: Priya Nair (design soft-fence, burst decay)
- Scheduler/Allocator: Liam Patel (autocalibration, lock removal) [assignee]
- Observability: Marco Ruiz (dashboards, alerts)
- Testing infra: Zoe Kim (synthetic harness + CI jobs)
- Capacity analytics: Elias Novak (policy simulation)

Deliverables and timeline:
- Week 1: lightweight synthetic harness prototype + initial dashboard (MVP).
- Week 2-3: autocalibration prototype in staging (dry-run logs) + lock removal POC.
- Week 4: deploy soft-fence + enable CI synthetic test; measure impact with 2-week canary.

Notes and links:
- Postmortem: https://confluence.redwood.ai/dedicated-capacity-postmortem-2025-02-10
- Incident Grafana: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/alerts/dedicated-capacity-2025-02-10
- Design doc (prototype harness): https://drive.redwood.ai/drive/folders/synthetic-load-validator-proto
- Related tickets: ENG-29315 (spike-mitigation-autotuner), ENG-52489 (reservation policy review), ENG-41322 (burst calibration)

title: Mailbox probe + drift-aware adaptive discard for low-cost trace capture
preview:
Background: Our current tracing hotpath collects a large volume of spans for inference requests which increases tail allocations and CPU in the hotpath. Existing work reduced noise via prefilters and deferred emission, but we still see cases where small bursts and model drift cause missed high-value traces or unnecessary allocations for low-value traces.

Goal: Implement a lightweight, mailbox-style probe that opportunistically samples contextual headers and a drift-aware adaptive discard stage that drops low-value tracing tokens outside the mailbox budget. The mailbox probe must add near-zero allocations in the hotpath and enable accurate tail reconstruction (posthoc) while keeping overall tracer CPU and memory overhead under the target SLO (<=0.5% CPU on inference path).

Approach:
1) Mailbox probe (hotpath): implement a tiny fixed-size circular metadata mailbox per worker thread that stores a compact fingerprint for a small set of recent requests (request-id hash, route id, short latency bucket). Writes are single atomic CAS-free stores of 64-bit entries to avoid allocations. The probe records only when a request enters the critical path and only for a randomized 0.1% of requests by default (configurable per-tenant).

2) Drift-aware adaptive discard (background): a background controller consumes mailbox entries and applies a lightweight scoring model that considers recent latency histograms, route importance, and a drift signal from model output rate changes. The controller decides to retain full spans for a bounded budget or replace them with compact provenance tokens that allow later reconstruction when paired with sample-forwarded headers.

3) Graceful downgrade and reconstruction: when budget is exceeded, the collector will downgrade detailed span emission to provenance tokens with a minimal retained context that allows targeted rehydration (via request replay on shadow traces or by requesting logs from the request owner). The rehydration pipeline runs offline to reconstruct tails for postmortem analyses.

4) Telemetry and rollouts: expose per-worker mailbox occupancy, budget burn rate, reconstructed-tail hit-rate, and hotpath allocation/latency gauges as metrics. Add dashboard panels and alerts for mailbox saturation and reconstruction failure rates.

Implementation details:
- Runtime: change lives in inference-server worker: add Mailbox struct, background controller in tracer manager service. Code lives in repo: https://github.com/redwood-inference/inference-runtime (WIP branch mailbox-probe).
- PRs: initial hotpath micro-optimized write: https://github.com/redwood-inference/inference-runtime/pull/2345 (draft). Background controller PR planned after hotpath tests.
- Config: per-tenant sampling_budget_pct, mailbox_size (default 64 entries per worker), drift_window_secs (default 300s). Expose via runtime config and Redwood Console feature flag for gradual rollout.

Testing plan:
- Unit tests for mailbox write/read semantics and zero-alloc hotpath invariants.
- Microbenchmarks: measure per-request allocation, CPU impact and tail latency with and without probe under 100k qps synthetic load. Target <= 100ns added p99 latency and <=0.5% CPU overhead.
- Canary rollout: enable for a small set of tenants (internal orgs + 3 pilot customers) with verbose logging and reconstruction enabled. Monitor mailbox metrics and reconstruction success for 48h before wider enablement.

Acceptance criteria:
- Hotpath implementation demonstrates <256B extra RSS per worker and <0.5% CPU increase in production-like load tests.
- Mailbox telemetry exposed and dashboard created in Console.
- Background controller correctly rehydrates >=90% of high-latency tails in the canary tenants.
- New PRs merged and feature behind feature-flag for gradual rollouts.

title: Tenant credit-based region routing with variant shadowing and graceful hosted backfill
preview:
Summary:
Design and implement a tenant credit allocator that influences region & tier selection for dedicated capacity while enabling controlled hosted spillback. The allocator issues soft "credits" (soft reservations) based on pre-commit, historical consumption, and SLA tier. Credits drive probabilistic routing decisions: prefer dedicated when credits >= cost_estimate, gradually bleed to hosted when credits are low.

Goals:
- Reduce unexpected hosted spillovers for customers with reserved capacity while allowing controlled, observable backfill to hosted for short bursts.
- Maintain latency SLOs for P0/P1 tenants and keep unit cost predictable for reserved customers.
- Provide a transparent, debuggable fallback mechanism to hosted and to lower-cost model variants.

Design highlights:
1) Credit allocator
- Each tenant has a credit balance updated continuously: credits += reserved_rate * time_window - actual_consumption_cost. Credits are bounded and never drop below a soft negative floor.
- Credit tiers: GREEN (>75%), AMBER (25%-75%), RED (<25%). Routing preference score = base_affinity * f(credit_tier) * tenant_priority_scale.

2) Probabilistic routing with hysteresis
- For each request compute P(dedicated) = sigmoid((score - threshold)/temperature). Add request jitter and short-term moving-average smoothing to avoid flapping.
- Hysteresis windows vary by tier: GREEN (30s), AMBER (2m), RED (10m).

3) Variant shadowing and demotion
- If hosted or lower-cost variant is selected, send a shadow copy to a demoted variant for quality monitoring (no charge to tenant). Shadowing includes: prompt/response hashing, deterministic knob to redact sensitive fields, and TTL for cache.
- Maintain per-variant quality deltas; if demoted variant drops below quality delta threshold for >N requests, automatically escalate to human-in-the-loop review and revert demotion if necessary.

4) Graceful hosted backfill policy
- Backfill is allowed for short bursts if predicted dedicated wait time > backfill_threshold and tenant in RED/AMBER. Backfill must carry metadata (origin:dedicated-backfill, credit_balance, fallback_decision_id) for observability and billing reconciliation.
- Introduce a backfill budget per-tenant per-day to limit cost exposure from hosted spillover.

5) Preemption & recovery
- Dedicated slots are preemptible by a request to reclaim credits: reclaiming triggers a soft-fence preventing re-routing to hosted for a brief cooldown, and emits an event to the tenant alerting them to degraded capacity.
- Recovery path replenishes credits based on committed onboarding schedule and usage smoothing.

Telemetry & observability:
- Emit these metrics at request-level and aggregated: routing_decision(reason), credit_balance_gauge, time_in_tier, backfill_event_count, variant_quality_delta, failed_shadow_rate, cost_delta_per_hour.
- Dashboards: tenant-level SLO heatmap, backfill budget burn-rate, variant-regression alerts.
- Traceable IDs: every decision must surface fallback_decision_id and policy_version in logs to enable postmortems.

Acceptance criteria:
- Simulated 48-hour stress test shows >60% reduction in unexpected hosted spillovers for tenants with >50% reserved commits compared to baseline routing.
- No increase in P0 99th p99 latency beyond 5% during simulated burst tests.
- Integration tests for credit accounting, backfill tagging, and variant shadowing coverage >90%.

Rollout plan:
1) Canary: enable credit allocator for 2 internal tenants and one low-risk external tenant (1 week).
2) Monitoring: create dashboards and an automated guardrail that disables backfill when budget burn-rate exceeds 120% of expected.
3) Progressive exposure: +10% of tenants per day with automated rollback on regression.
4) Post-rollout: 2-week observation then adjust tier thresholds and hysteresis windows based on real traffic.

Open questions / tradeoffs:
- How to reconcile multi-region commitments where tenant reserves span multiple hemisheres? Proposal: maintain per-region credits with cross-region transfer cap. This adds complexity but preserves locality.
- Determining cost_estimate for long-running streaming requests. We propose a moving-window model that predicts token consumption after N tokens.
- Privacy: shadowing preserves request fingerprints but must redact PII; integrate with KMS and redact rules from private deployments.

Links & artifacts:
- Design doc (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=7682002
- Initial simulation notebook: https://github.com/redwood-inference/routing-simulations/tree/tenant-credit-experiments
- Draft PR (routing service): https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/5312
- Runbook stub: https://wiki.redwood.ai/runbooks/dedicated-credit-backfill-runbook

- Traceable IDs: every decision must surface fallback_decision_id and policy_version in logs to enable postmortems.

Acceptance criteria:
- Simulated 48-hour stress test shows >60% reduction in unexpected hosted spillovers for tenants with >50% reserved commits compared to baseline routing.
- No increase in P0 99th p99 latency beyond 5% during simulated burst tests.
- Integration tests for credit accounting, backfill tagging, and variant shadowing coverage >90%.

Rollout plan:
1) Canary: enable credit allocator for 2 internal tenants and one low-risk external tenant (1 week).
2) Monitoring: create dashboards and an automated guardrail that disables backfill when budget burn-rate exceeds 120% of expected.
3) Progressive exposure: +10% of tenants per day with automated rollback on regression.
4) Post-rollout: 2-week observation then adjust tier thresholds and hysteresis windows based on real traffic.

Open questions / tradeoffs:
- How to reconcile multi-region commitments where tenant reserves span multiple hemisheres? Proposal: maintain per-region credits with cross-region transfer cap. This adds complexity but preserves locality.
- Determining cost_estimate for long-running streaming requests. We propose a moving-window model that predicts token consumption after N tokens.
- Privacy: shadowing preserves request fingerprints but must redact PII; integrate with KMS and redact rules from private deployments.

Links & artifacts:
- Design doc (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=7682002
- Initial simulation notebook: https://github.com/redwood-inference/routing-simulations/tree/tenant-credit-experiments
- Draft PR (routing service): https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/5312
- Runbook stub: https://wiki.redwood.ai/runbooks/dedicated-credit-backfill-runbook

title: Adaptive Pool Tuner: Predictive Cost & Latency Tradeoff Canvas
preview:
Problem: Operators of dedicated GPU pools need a fast way to tune autoscale and burst settings while understanding the expected cost and latency tradeoffs for the observed workload. Current pool controls are distributed across multiple panels; users must toggle between health, scaling policy, and cost dashboards to make a single configuration change. This leads to conservative settings, overprovisioning, and surprise bills.

Goal: Design a single, compact canvas — the Adaptive Pool Tuner — that surfaces predicted tail latency, median latency, and incremental cost for proposed autoscale and burst rule changes. The canvas should support short iterative workflows: propose change, simulate effect on a chosen workload profile, preview cost delta and SLO impact, save as draft or apply with rollout policy.

Scope:
- A compact left rail with workload selector (recent routes, custom trace upload).
- Central visualization showing current vs. simulated latency percentiles and throughput at configurable time slices.
- Right rail cost summary with per-hour and per-24h delta, and suggestions (e.g., reduce max-burst, increase cold-scale delay).
- Inline canary rollout controls: percent, duration, automatic rollback threshold.
- Export links to runbook and annotated snapshot for on-call.

Out of scope for this ticket: backend simulation engine; we will use a mocked predictive API for prototype. Deeper cost-model accuracy and multi-pool orchestration will be follow-ups.

Success metrics:
- Reduce number of console pages visited per tuning session to 1 (tracked via analytics).
- 30% fewer manual scaling overrides per pool in the first 90 days after rollout (pilot customers).
- Time-to-decision for ops engineers < 3 minutes for common tuning tasks.

Design constraints & considerations:
- Must be usable on 1280px width with collapsed left nav (compact mode).
- Accessibility: color contrast for visual bands, keyboard navigable controls, aria labels for simulation chart nodes.
- Mobile: view-only mode for on-call responders (not a full edit flow).
- Trust: show confidence band in simulations and a short note when predictions rely on sampled traces.

Deliverables:
- Low-fidelity flows (edge cases: high-variance workloads, sudden spike simulation).
- High-fidelity Figma prototype with interactive simulation slider and rollout config.
- Design spec (tokens, spacing, microcopy) and annotated accessibility checklist.
- Usability test plan for 6 internal operators + 2 pilot customers.

Links to artifacts: Figma: https://www.figma.com/file/EXAMPLE/adaptive-pool-tuner (prototype v1) | Confluence spec: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INF/Adaptive+Pool+Tuner | Mock API for sim: https://github.com/redwood-inference/pool-sim-mock/pull/14

title: Signal Flow Navigator — lineage and lightweight request exploration
preview:
Problem: Engineers need a fast, low-friction way to explore request lineage across services without the cognitive overhead of a full-forensics trace workspace. Current consoles focus on dense timelines and span-level forensic tools; customers asked for a lightweight explorer that surfaces end-to-end causality, correlation IDs, and cost/latency overlays for quick debugging and rollout validation.

Goal: Design a compact, keyboard-first 'Signal Flow Navigator' that lets users: 1) search by correlation ID or route, 2) see a condensed lineage graph + linear time-strip, 3) toggle span-grouping and sampling, and 4) overlay per-request cost and latency heatmarks. It must integrate with rollouts so PM/SEs can validate canaries and quickly escalate or rollback.

Scope: UX surface + clickable prototype (Figma) for console team review; include acceptance criteria, accessibility considerations, telemetry events to track, and handoff notes for frontend engineering. Not in scope: backend sampling pipeline changes (will be tracked as ENG deps).

title: Latency-Adaptive Phase Sharding with Kernel-Cost Feedback
preview:
Problem: In mixed workloads our current phase-splitting heuristics are either too aggressive (increasing overhead and hurting throughput) or too conservative (causing p95/p99 latency spikes for low-latency requests). We lack a tight feedback loop from kernel-level cost signals (measured FLOPs, attention memory hotspots, quantization path selection) back into the scheduler, so the allocator cannot make fine-grained sharding decisions that balance latency and throughput at the token-phase level.

Goal: Introduce a scheduler policy that shards large prefill/decode phases into adaptive micro-shards using real-time kernel-cost feedback. The policy should: (1) reduce tail latency for latency-sensitive requests without materially lowering sustained throughput, (2) avoid pathological fragmentation overhead, and (3) be safe to roll out per-customer and per-route with canarying and automatic rollback thresholds.

title: Endpoint behavioral SLI spec and graceful capacity-shedding guidelines
preview: Create a pragmatic, behavioral SLI specification for model endpoints that maps to observable request behaviors (short vs long context, streaming vs non-streaming) and define a multi-tiered capacity-shedding policy that preserves high-priority traffic using graceful degradation, model fallback, regional evacuation, and circuit breakers. The aim is to give on-call and platform automation a clear decision tree to protect error budgets while minimizing customer-visible regressions.
text: Create a pragmatic, behavioral SLI specification for model endpoints that maps to observable request behaviors (short vs long context, streaming vs non-streaming) and define a multi-tiered capacity-shedding policy that preserves high-priority traffic using graceful degradation, model fallback, regional evacuation, and circuit breakers. The aim is to give on-call and platform automation a clear decision tree to protect error budgets while minimizing customer-visible regressions.

title: Token Waterfall Slate: compact attribution + export patterns
preview: Design and prototype a compact, high-density "Waterfall Slate" for the Redwood Console cost dashboard that surfaces token-level attribution (per-route, per-model, per-invocation) and supports multi-format export. The slate should present a layered token-cost waterfall (origin layers: prompt, completion, KV cache hit, prefix reuse, retries), allow quick pivoting by dimension (route, workspace, model family, quantization profile), and provide guided pricing-simulation controls (unit rate overrides, slab presets, reserved capacity adjustments). The goal is a single-pane summary that is readable for both engineers and finance stakeholders, with progressive disclosure for forensic detail and an export format suited for downstream chargeback and BI ingestion.

title: Adaptive FlowCards and Responsive Token Synthesis for Rollout Console
preview: Objective: Create a single FlowCard component family and a responsive token synthesis approach that adapts density, contrast, and motion across rollouts dashboards. The goal is to reduce component sprawl in the Console, enable consistent canary/A-B rollout UIs, and make token-driven behavior (spacing, elevation, animation duration) predictable for engineering handoff. This ticket drives a Figma library delivery, accessibility pass, and a small prototype to validate interaction under live data loads.

title: Catalog pricing matrix + deprecation banners — screenshot & quickstart visual pack for release notes
preview: Produce a focused set of UX assets and high-fidelity screenshots for the Feb catalog release notes: primary deliverables are a pricing matrix visualization (desktop + mobile + dark mode), model variant rows (standard/quantized/fast), deprecation banners and the migration quickstart modal. These will be used in the public release notes, the Console release banner, and the onboarding quickstart flow that surfaces newly added or deprecated models. The designs must be consistent with the refreshed catalog visual language and include explicit cost/tooling callouts (per-token and estimated hour cost), region availability badges, and an accessible deprecation CTA flow.

title: Dark-mode interaction ink reflow and graph legend hierarchy
preview: Problem: In deep-dark console themes interactive elements (buttons, menus, toggles) exhibit visual artifacts and perceived weight due to 'ink' reflow and overlay rules. Chart legends and color keys lose hierarchy against near-black surfaces, and dense tables depend on thin separators that drop below readable contrast at small sizes. Scope: Create a token mapping and component override proposal for deep/dense dark surfaces that preserves accessible contrast, preserves brand accent identity, and avoids visual clutter across tables, charts, and controls. Deliverables: 1) Token transform rules (alpha overlays, elevation-strength multipliers, accent-saturation clamp) 2) Component override spec for Button / Menu / Toggle / Table row / Chart legend 3) Figma tokens and component variants 4) Implementation guidance for theme engine (runtime mapping + opt-in canary) 5) Automated contrast tests and visual regression sets.

Deliverables: 1) Token transform rules (alpha overlays, elevation-strength multipliers, accent-saturation clamp) 2) Component override spec for Button / Menu / Toggle / Table row / Chart legend 3) Figma tokens and component variants 4) Implementation guidance for theme engine (runtime mapping + opt-in canary) 5) Automated contrast tests and visual regression sets.

title: Incident-aware Upgrade Schedule Editor for Private Clusters
preview:
Problem: Operators of private/VPC clusters need a single, actionable place in the Console to schedule cluster upgrades while taking active incidents, maintenance windows, and customer SLOs into account. Current upgrade scheduling is too static: it doesn't show ongoing incidents, doesn't suggest safe canary windows, and lacks a clear rollback/abort affordance for mid-upgrade incidents.

Goal: Design an interactive Upgrade Schedule Editor that: 1) surfaces current incident state and cluster health when picking times; 2) suggests safe windows based on SLA/SLO and past busy-hours telemetry; 3) supports canary waves, staged rollouts, and quick abort/rollback actions; 4) integrates RBAC so only authorized personas can change critical windows; and 5) provides clear audit trail + links to incident runbooks.

Scope of this ticket: UX prototypes and validation for the Editor (desktop & compact tablet widths), microcopy for alerts/warnings, edge-case flows (ongoing incident, multiple clusters in a pool, cross-region dependencies), and final handoff assets for engineering. Not in scope: backend scheduling engine work, feature flagging logic, or on-prem installer changes (those are separate ENG tickets).

Key user stories:
- As an SRE, I want the editor to warn me if an active incident affects >10% of nodes so I can delay the upgrade.
- As a Platform PM, I want suggested canary windows that minimize customer impact based on historical peak hours.
- As an Operator, I want a single-click abort that stops rollout and triggers rollback playbooks.
- As an Admin, I want audit metadata (who scheduled, what changed) surfaced in the timeline and exportable as CSV.

title: Privileged role grant review & audit UX for Console
preview:
Problem: Admins need a clear, low-friction way to grant, review, and revoke privileged roles (e.g., system-admin, billing-admin, oncall-privileged) while ensuring every change is auditable and can be justified for compliance. Current console path buries audit metadata and the member assignment flow creates accidental over-grants because scopes and resource boundaries are not surfaced.

Goal: Design a compact role grant review surface in Console that: 1) surfaces resource scoping and active sessions for the member being assigned; 2) flags high-risk grants with inline justifications and mandatory commit notes for P0/P1 roles; 3) integrates with audit log links and one-click revoke/impersonate for validation; 4) preserves quick bulk workflows for SRE/platform teams while preventing accidental permanent elevation.

Scope: UI/UX for role editor and member assignment flows only (Console). Non-goals: backend policy engine changes, SSO mapping logic, or RBAC enforcement semantics (these are separate eng tickets).

Key UX principles: minimal friction for routine ops, mandatory friction for high-risk actions, continuous discoverability of audit context, and clear affordances for temporary grants and review windows.

title: Onboarding network topology journey frames
preview: Create a set of visual assets and annotated UI frames that guide operators through Private/Private-network onboarding end-to-end: topology overview, initial install steps, control-plane handshake, RBAC & key setup, and in-place upgrade flows. Assets will be used in the Console onboarding flow, docs quickstart, and release notes for runtime-1.18.

Assets will be used in the Console onboarding flow, docs quickstart, and release notes for runtime-1.18.

title: Pricing commitment matrix & security questionnaire alignment for SSO/RBAC contract variability
preview: Run a pricing/packaging experiment and create canonical security-questionnaire answers tying SSO (SAML/SCIM) and RBAC commitments to contractual packaging. Goal: reduce negotiation friction, standardize commitments across tiers, and measure lift in close rate and deal size when we offer clearly-scoped SSO/RBAC commitments vs bespoke offers.

Goal: reduce negotiation friction, standardize commitments across tiers, and measure lift in close rate and deal size when we offer clearly-scoped SSO/RBAC commitments vs bespoke offers.

title: Batching & Cache Setup Wizard — onboarding flow and safety guardrails
preview: Design an end-to-end onboarding wizard inside Redwood Console that helps engineers and platform owners configure batching and KV/prefix caching for routes and model profiles. The wizard should: 1) quickly recommend safe defaults based on measured workload signals (median seq len, token rate, request size distribution), 2) explain trade-offs (latency vs cost vs variance) with short contextual explainers, 3) provide a simulated cost & latency preview for chosen presets and slider settings, 4) expose advanced controls in a scoped ‘expert’ panel with JSON preview and validation, and 5) enforce guardrails and warnings for settings that would violate SLOs or exceed estimated spend. This ticket covers the visual flows, microcopy, onboarding checklist, one progressive disclosure prototype in Figma, and a short pilot usability test with 6 backend engineers and 4 ML infra users.

title: Surface-elevation press shadows and RTL icon offsets: cross-theme button parity
preview: Audit and implement a coherent approach to button press feedback (shadows/contrast) that respects surface elevation tokens across light, dark, and high-contrast themes, and fix inconsistent RTL icon offsets and hit targets introduced during the recent token migration. Deliver a small set of token mappings, design specs, and a developer-friendly migration guide so engineering can implement with minimal regressions.

Deliver a small set of token mappings, design specs, and a developer-friendly migration guide so engineering can implement with minimal regressions.

title: Performance Signalboard: model & route overview canvas
preview:
Goal: Design a compact, scannable ‘Performance Signalboard’ canvas that surfaces per-model and per-route runtime signals (latency percentiles, throughput, token metrics, cost per token) for devs and platform engineers. The canvas should work as a route-level default view and be embeddable in model pages and rollout screens.

Scope:
- Primary surface: single-row cards for routes/models with sparkline + trend + KPI badges.
- Expandable detail panel to show percentile charts (p50/p95/p99), tokens-per-call distribution, and request concurrency heatmap.
- Cost overlay that estimates per-1k tokens and per-1000-requests for the selected timeframe.
- Rollout annotations: highlight canary windows and automatic fallback events on timelines.
- Interaction patterns: hover tooltips, time-range zoom, selectable percentile lines, and quick copy of telemetry queries.

Out of scope for this ticket: in-console alerting workflow, RBAC control surfaces, and long-retention archival views (handled by Console Observability team).

Acceptance criteria:
- High-fidelity Figma prototype for desktop (1200–1600px) with responsive collapse behavior.
- Chart specs document that details data columns, downsampling, 1s/5s/1m aggregation windows and SLO shading rules.
- Annotated handoff page for frontend (components, CSS tokens, ARIA hints) and telemetry (recommended PromQL/SQL snippets).
- Sign-off from PM, Eng (runtime infra), and Research (metrics validation).
